remarks.title: Making Development Finance, Trade and Partnerships Work Harder for Africa

speaker.name: María del Pilar Garrido Gonzalo
speaker.title: Director for Development Co-operation
speaker.organization: Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD)

summary.executive_summary: María del Pilar Garrido Gonzalo described a global development environment marked by shifting alliances, dispersed power, rewritten rules for trade and investment, supply-chain shocks, weaker growth, and sharply declining official development assistance. These pressures are especially severe for developing countries with limited fiscal space and high exposure to food and energy costs. She argued that trade, investment, and development cooperation must no longer be treated separately, but as connected tools for creating jobs, reducing external dependence, and strengthening resilience. While acknowledging the severity of financing constraints, she rejected pessimism and called for innovation in how concessional finance and other development resources are prioritized and deployed. Africa’s critical minerals, young population, expanding workforce, and African Continental Free Trade Area were presented as foundations for greater economic agency. Garrido stressed that trade policy must remain centered on people and development outcomes. She also highlighted the value of the Global Network of Chief Economists and the African Chief Economists Network as spaces for evidence-based peer exchange. Her concluding message was that international partners should not prescribe solutions, but support African-led conversations with evidence, tools, learning, and sustained partnership.
summary.purpose: To assess the implications of global fragmentation and declining development finance, call for more innovative and effective use of concessional resources, and reaffirm the OECD’s support for African-led trade, resilience, and policy cooperation.

main_themes.theme: A changing and uncertain global order
main_themes.description: International cooperation is being reshaped by dispersed power, shifting alliances, rewritten trade and finance rules, supply-chain shocks, and weaker global growth.
Official development assistance has fallen sharply, creating particularly severe consequences for countries that depend heavily on concessional resources.

main_themes.theme: Integrated trade, investment and development cooperation
main_themes.description: Trade, investment, and development cooperation should function as complementary parts of a single strategy for jobs, resilience, and reduced external dependence.
Critical minerals, a young and growing workforce, and the AfCFTA give Africa strong foundations for productive transformation and more diversified partnerships.
Trade policy must remain focused on people and should help countries participate in and benefit from international trade.
Chief economist networks can connect international, regional, and national expertise without prescribing answers, while supporting African-led policy dialogue.

key_messages.message: Global economic fragmentation and weaker growth are increasing food, energy, fiscal, and social pressures across developing countries.
Trade, investment, and development cooperation should be treated as interconnected tools for building resilient, job-creating economies.
The decline in official development assistance requires better prioritization and more effective use of all forms of development finance.
Future concessional-finance allocations should work better and harder for Africa, rather than merely operating around it.
Africa’s critical minerals, youthful population, growing workforce, and continental market create substantial potential for resilience and agency.
International cooperation should bring evidence, tools, and partnership to African-led conversations rather than prescribing solutions.

priorities_and_challenges: Responding to weaker global growth, trade fragmentation, and repeated supply-chain shocks.
Protecting vulnerable populations from rising food and energy costs.
Managing the sharp decline in official development assistance.
Making concessional finance and other development resources more effective.
Using trade and investment to create jobs and reduce external dependence.
Building stronger African-led networks for policy evidence and peer exchange.
commitments_and_announcements: The OECD reaffirmed its commitment to partnering with African institutions and supporting African ambitions.
The OECD convened the Global Network of Chief Economists of International Development Agencies and Finance Institutions alongside the conference.
The speaker highlighted the inaugural meeting of the African Chief Economists Network as an important milestone.
The OECD committed to contribute evidence, tools, and partnership rather than prescribe policy answers.
policy_implications: Development partners should prioritize and protect official development assistance where needs and vulnerabilities are greatest.
Concessional and other development finance should be assessed according to their development impact.
Trade, investment, and development-cooperation policies should be designed as a coherent package.
Trade policy should support productive transformation, employment, resilience, and diversified partnerships.
African-led policy networks should be strengthened to improve peer learning and evidence-based decision-making.
International partners should align support with African priorities and locally led policy conversations.
calls_to_action: Show what innovation and change should look like for Africa and on Africa’s terms.
Make future concessional-finance allocations work better and harder for Africa.
Protect and prioritize official development assistance.
Use all forms of development finance more effectively.
Keep people and development outcomes at the center of trade policy.
Strengthen peer exchange among African and international economic-policy institutions.
partnerships_and_stakeholders: Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development
Global Network of Chief Economists of International Development Agencies and Finance Institutions
summary.concluding_message: The OECD stands ready to support African-led ambitions through partnership, evidence, tools, and peer learning. The objective is not to prescribe answers, but to help ensure that trade, development finance, and international cooperation strengthen Africa’s resilience and agency.

powerful_quotes.quote: Business as usual is no longer possible, nor wanted.
powerful_quotes.context: Calling for innovation in development finance and international cooperation.

powerful_quotes.quote: It is about people. It is always about people.
powerful_quotes.context: Emphasizing that trade policy must remain centered on human development.

powerful_quotes.quote: Not to prescribe answers, but to bring evidence, tools, and partnership.
powerful_quotes.context: Defining the appropriate role of international partners in African policy dialogue.

powerful_quotes.quote: We are here to see how we can best support you in that ambition.
powerful_quotes.context: Reaffirming the OECD’s partnership with African institutions.

highlights.text: The OECD revised its global growth projection to 2.8% for 2026.
Official development assistance reportedly fell by 23% in 2025, the sharpest decline since OECD measurement began in 1960.
Africa’s critical minerals, youthful population, expanding workforce, and AfCFTA market were identified as major strategic assets.
The OECD–WTO Aid for Trade Initiative was said to have mobilized $51 billion in 2024.
The African Chief Economists Network was highlighted as a milestone for practical peer exchange and African-led policy dialogue.
International partners were urged to support African conversations with evidence and tools instead of prescribing solutions.

uncertain_items: Several names and titles in the opening acknowledgements are mistranscribed.
The phrase transcribed as 'part of the EU' near 00:00:23 is unclear and was excluded.
The transcript uses 'OGA' where the intended term is clearly 'ODA' or official development assistance.
The statement describing a 23% fall in ODA also mentions 'nearly $1 billion,' which appears inconsistent or incomplete and was not repeated outside the general decline.
The exact projected ODA levels for sub-Saharan Africa and least developed countries near 00:02:21 are unintelligible.
The phrase near 00:03:19 was interpreted as 'for Africa and on Africa’s terms,' but the final word is poorly transcribed.
The name of the OECD–WTO Aid for Trade Initiative is imperfectly transcribed but clear from context.
The exact date of the remarks is not stated in the transcript.
